plain_definition: The member-state-level regulator that actually authorises and supervises an individual crypto-asset service provider (CASP) or asset-referenced/e-money token issuer under MiCA -- for example, a country's own securities regulator or central bank. MiCA, the DLT Pilot Regime, and the crypto travel rule are all EU-level Regulations that set the rulebook, but the actual licensing decision, day-to-day supervision, and any enforcement action against a named firm are acts of the relevant NCA under its own national administrative procedure, not of ESMA, EBA, or the European Commission. Once one member state's NCA authorises a CASP, that authorisation passports across the whole EU. This is a genuine scope limitation for this radar: because this jurisdiction's coverage is EU-level only, individual NCA authorisation decisions, national supervisory practice, and enforcement against named firms are out of scope of the cards and pillar states this project publishes for the EU -- the radar tracks the Regulation itself, Commission delegated/implementing acts, and ESMA/EBA/AMLA technical standards and guidelines, not any of the 27 member states' own regulators' case-by-case decisions.
formal_definition: MiCA (Regulation (EU) 2023/1114) requires each member state to designate one or more national competent authorities responsible for authorising and supervising crypto-asset service providers under Title V and asset-referenced/e-money token issuers under Titles III-IV; NCA identity and structure vary by member state and are not tracked individually by this EU-level-only jurisdiction.
